items.question_text: Complete: Where control responsibility within Canadian airspace has been formally delegated to the FAA by the _____, apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures contained in this chapter.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-1 body, the delegating entity is the Transport Canada Aviation Group.

choices.text: Transport Canada Aviation Group

items.question_text: Per the note in 12-1-1, in what year did the U.S. and Canada establish the agreement recognizing the inherent safety of each other's ATC procedures?
items.explanation: The note in 12-1-1 states that the agreement was established in 1985.

items.question_text: According to the note in 12-1-1, what limits the ability of one country to use its own ATC procedures in the airspace over the territory of the other country?
items.explanation: The note states procedures of one country may be used insofar as they are not inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the laws and regulations or unique operational requirements of the country over whose territory the airspace is located.

choices.text: The procedures must not be inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the laws and regulations or unique operational requirements of the country over whose territory the airspace is located.
The procedures are limited only by the equipment available at the controlling facility, regardless of the host country's laws.
Each country may use its procedures unconditionally because the agreement is a blanket permission with no restrictions.
The procedures are limited to IFR operations only, with VFR governed exclusively by the host country's regulations.

items.question_text: Per the note in 12-1-1, the 1985 U.S.-Canada agreement was revised to include ALL Canadian ATC procedures, regardless of whether they conflict with U.S. regulations.
items.explanation: Per the note, Chapter 12 was revised to include only those Canadian procedures that must be used because of a Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirement — not all Canadian procedures.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete: Canadian Class A airspace is designated from the base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including _____.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2a, Class A airspace extends up to and including FL 600.

choices.text: FL 600

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, Canadian Class B airspace includes all controlled low level airspace above what lower boundary, and to what upper boundary?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, Class B includes low level airspace above 12,500 feet ASL or at and above the minimum en route IFR altitude (whichever is higher), up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL.

choices.text: Above 10,000 feet ASL up to but not including FL 180.
Above 12,500 feet ASL or the MEA (whichever is higher), up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL.
Above 14,500 feet MSL up to and including FL 230.
Above the transition altitude, up to but not including FL 600.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, which Canadian airspace classes have a requirement that VFR flights must obtain an ATC clearance to enter?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2c, Class C airspace requires a clearance from ATC for VFR entry. Class D requires two-way communications, not a clearance. Classes A and B don't permit ordinary VFR at all.

choices.text: Classes C and D.
Class C only.
Classes B and C.
Classes A, B, and C.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, what is the correct pairing of Canadian airspace class and the VFR entry requirement?
items.explanation: Class C: clearance required. Class D: two-way communications required. Class E: no special requirements. Class A permits only IFR. Class B permits only IFR and CVFR (not ordinary VFR).

choices.text: Class D: ATC clearance required; Class E: two-way communications required.
Class C: ATC clearance required; Class D: two-way communications required; Class E: no special requirements.
Class C: two-way communications required; Class D: no special requirements; Class E: ATC clearance required.
Class C: no special requirements; Class D: ATC clearance required; Class E: two-way communications required.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, into which two sub-types may Canadian Class F airspace (special use airspace) be classified?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2f, special use airspace may be classified as Class F advisory or Class F restricted.

choices.text: Class F advisory or Class F restricted
Class F advisory, Class F restricted

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, Canadian Class G airspace is uncontrolled airspace within which ATC has neither the authority nor responsibility for exercising control over air traffic.
items.explanation: This is the verbatim definition of Class G airspace from 12-1-2g.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-2, what is the CVFR pilot responsible for maintaining at all times?
items.explanation: Per the note, the CVFR pilot is responsible to maintain VFR flight and visual reference to the ground at all times — even though ATC applies IFR separation procedures to the aircraft.

choices.text: Two-way radio communications on a discrete frequency designated by ATC.
VFR flight and visual reference to the ground at all times.
A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical separation from all other IFR traffic.
The last assigned IFR altitude without deviation, regardless of cloud clearance needs.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 12-1-3, clear an aircraft to maintain 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top' in lieu of '_____', provided the pilot requests it.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-3, the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance is used in lieu of VFR-on-top.

choices.text: VFR-on-top

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-3, what are the two weather conditions that must be met for a 1,000-feet-on-top operation per the pilot's responsibility?
items.explanation: Per the note, the pilot must ensure the operation can be conducted at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with a flight visibility of 3 miles or more.

choices.text: At least 500 feet above all cloud and flight visibility of 5 miles or more.
At least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with flight visibility of 3 miles or more.
At least 2,000 feet above the highest terrain within 25 NM and flight visibility of 1 mile or more.
Clear of cloud with at least 1,000 feet ceiling and flight visibility of at least 1 mile.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-3, which of the following airspace prohibitions blocks the use of the 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top' clearance?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-3b, the pilot must not operate within Class A or Class B airspace. Class C, D, E, F, and G are not prohibited by this paragraph.

choices.text: The pilot will not operate within Class C or Class D airspace.
The pilot will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.
The pilot will not operate within Class A, B, or C airspace.
The pilot will not operate within any controlled airspace.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-3, a controller must independently verify that flight visibility is 3 miles or more before approving a 1,000-feet-on-top request.
items.explanation: Per the note, it is the pilot's responsibility to ensure the conditions are adequate, and the pilot's request can be considered as confirmation that conditions are adequate. The controller does not independently verify weather.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, between which categories of aircraft must a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum be applied?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, the separation minimum applies between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance. CVFR aircraft receive IFR-equivalent separation treatment.

choices.text: Aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance.
Only aircraft operating under an IFR clearance; CVFR aircraft self-separate.
All aircraft regardless of clearance type, including ordinary VFR traffic.
Only turbine-powered aircraft on IFR clearances; piston aircraft on CVFR are excluded.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, does a CVFR aircraft in excellent VMC receive the same separation minimum as an IFR aircraft in IMC?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, separation is applied to aircraft on IFR or CVFR clearances regardless of the weather conditions. CVFR in VMC still receives the same separation minimum.

choices.text: No — CVFR aircraft in VMC may rely on see-and-avoid instead of ATC separation.
Yes — the separation minimum applies regardless of weather conditions, so CVFR aircraft in VMC receive the same protection as IFR aircraft in IMC.
Yes — but only vertical separation applies to CVFR aircraft; lateral and longitudinal minima do not.
No — CVFR aircraft are treated like ordinary VFR traffic when weather permits.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, only vertical separation, but not lateral or longitudinal, applies to aircraft operating under a CVFR clearance.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, apply a lateral, longitudinal, OR vertical separation minimum between aircraft on an IFR or CVFR clearance. Any one type suffices — the paragraph does not restrict which type applies to CVFR.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, which aircraft types are subject to the Canadian radio-failure altitude procedure that requires maintaining the last assigned altitude until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the procedure applies specifically to turbine-powered (turboprop or turbojet) aircraft. It does not describe the procedure for non-turbine aircraft in the paragraph.

choices.text: All aircraft operating in Canadian airspace, regardless of engine type.
Turbine-powered (turboprop or turbojet) aircraft.
Only turbojet aircraft; turboprops follow the standard U.S. 91.185 rule.
All IFR aircraft, but only when operating above FL 180.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 12-1-5, when a turbine-powered aircraft with a communication failure was cleared on departure to a point other than the destination, the pilot maintains the last assigned altitude or flight level or the _____, whichever is higher.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the altitude to maintain is the last assigned altitude/flight level or the minimum en route IFR altitude, whichever is higher.

choices.text: minimum en route IFR altitude
MEA

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, which condition overrides the standard Canadian radio-failure altitude procedure for a turbine-powered aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the procedure applies 'except when issued alternate radio failure instructions by ATC.'

choices.text: ATC has issued alternate radio failure instructions.
The pilot has filed an alternate airport in the flight plan.
The weather is VFR at the destination.
The aircraft is equipped with a secondary radio that remains operational.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, when a turbine aircraft will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit, when does the climb to the border-crossing altitude commence?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the climb is commenced at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — not at the clearance limit, not 10 minutes after, and not after entering U.S. airspace.

choices.text: Immediately upon passing the clearance limit, regardless of distance to the border.
10 minutes after passing the clearance limit.
At the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.
Only after the aircraft has entered U.S. airspace and established communications with the receiving facility.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, the Canadian radio-failure procedure applies to a turbine-powered aircraft cleared on departure to a point other than the destination. What does the pilot do after the 10-minute period beyond the clearance limit?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, after maintaining altitude until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, the pilot proceeds at altitude(s) or flight level(s) filed in the flight plan.

choices.text: Proceed at altitude(s) or flight level(s) filed in the flight plan.
Descend to the MEA for the remainder of the route.
Maintain the clearance-limit altitude all the way to the destination.
Climb to the highest altitude available in the flight levels for the route segment.

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-6, what form of authorization do Canadian regulations require for parachute operations?
items.explanation: Per the note, Canadian regulations require written authority from the Ministry of Transport.

choices.text: Verbal confirmation from the pilot on frequency.
Written authority from the Ministry of Transport.
A NOTAM filed at least 24 hours in advance.
An email from the airport manager to the controlling facility.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-6, from whom must prior permission be obtained before authorizing parachute jumping in Canadian airspace, and what specific entity does the note identify as the required source of written authority?
items.explanation: The body says 'the appropriate Canadian authority.' The note specifies that Canadian regulations require written authority from the Ministry of Transport. Together they identify the Ministry of Transport as the relevant Canadian authority.

choices.text: The body and note are independent; the body allows any Canadian aviation official, and the note is only advisory.
The body requires prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority, and the note identifies that authority as the Ministry of Transport requiring written form.
The body requires the pilot's prior permission, and the note requires written confirmation from the destination airport.
The body only requires the jump aircraft to be on an IFR flight plan, and the note clarifies the Ministry of Transport reviews the plan.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, how does the SVFR approval criteria differ between a control zone with an airport controller on duty versus a control zone without one?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7a, with a controller on duty, approve or refuse based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only. Per 12-1-7b, without a controller, base the decision on (1) current or anticipated IFR traffic AND (2) official ceiling and visibility reports.

choices.text: With a controller: IFR traffic only. Without a controller: IFR traffic only — the criteria are identical.
With a controller: IFR traffic only. Without a controller: both IFR traffic AND official ceiling and visibility reports.
With a controller: ceiling and visibility only. Without a controller: IFR traffic only.
With a controller: IFR traffic, ceiling, and visibility. Without a controller: IFR traffic only.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7c, what is the Canadian SVFR flight visibility minimum for aircraft other than helicopters?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7c1, aircraft other than helicopters require flight visibility (ground visibility when reported) of 1 mile.

choices.text: 1/2 mile flight visibility.
1 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when reported).
3 miles flight visibility.
2 miles flight visibility (ground visibility when reported).

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, when an airport controller is on duty and SVFR is approved, what additional specification must the controller provide?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7a, if approved, specify the period of time during which SVFR flight is permitted.

choices.text: Specify the period of time during which SVFR flight is permitted.
Specify the maximum altitude the aircraft may reach during the SVFR operation.
Specify a discrete transponder code for the duration of the SVFR clearance.
Specify a mandatory reporting point at the control zone boundary.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, when no airport controller is on duty, approving an SVFR request requires considering both IFR traffic and ceiling/visibility reports together. One factor alone is insufficient.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7b, without an airport controller on duty, the decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic AND official ceiling and visibility reports. Both factors must be considered.

choices.text: True
False

items.instruction: Identify the correct Canadian airspace class for the given description.
items.question_text: An airspace where only IFR and CVFR flights are permitted, extends from 12,500 feet ASL or MEA (whichever is higher) up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL, and ATC applies IFR procedures to CVFR aircraft.

choices.text: Class A airspace.
Class B airspace.
Class C airspace.
Class D airspace.

items.explanation: This matches the description of Canadian Class B airspace per 12-1-2b: IFR and CVFR only, vertical limits 12,500 ASL/MEA to below FL 180, with IFR procedures applied to CVFR aircraft.

items.situation: You have two aircraft in Canadian Class B airspace: one on an IFR clearance at 14,000 feet and one on a CVFR clearance at 13,500 feet. Another controller says you do not need to apply a separation minimum between them because the CVFR aircraft is not IFR and is responsible for maintaining visual reference to the ground.
items.question_text: How should separation be handled between these two aircraft?

choices.text: Apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum between them, because both are operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance, regardless of the weather conditions.
Let the CVFR pilot self-separate because CVFR means the pilot is responsible for seeing and avoiding IFR traffic.
Apply separation only if the CVFR pilot does not report the IFR traffic in sight.
Use only 500 feet vertical separation because the CVFR status reduces the required minimum by half.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, ATC procedures pertinent to IFR flights must be applied to CVFR aircraft. Per 12-1-4, a separation minimum applies to aircraft on IFR or CVFR clearances regardless of weather. The CVFR pilot's visual responsibility to the ground does not relieve ATC of the separation obligation.

items.situation: A pilot flying below FL 180 requests 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top.' The pilot confirms they will remain clear of Class A and Class B airspace. Another controller says to deny the request because you have not independently verified the ceiling is at least 1,000 feet or that visibility is 3 miles.
items.question_text: Should you approve or deny the request based on the controller's concern?

choices.text: Approve it if the pilot has requested it and will not operate in Class A or B. The pilot's request serves as confirmation that weather conditions are adequate.
Deny it because the controller must independently confirm ceiling and visibility before approving any 1,000-feet-on-top request.
Approve it only if the pilot additionally provides a PIREP confirming exactly 3 miles visibility.
Deny it because 1,000-feet-on-top is never permitted below FL 180 regardless of airspace class.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-3, the two requirements are (a) the pilot requests it and (b) the pilot will not operate in Class A or B. Per the note, weather adequacy is the pilot's responsibility and the request confirms conditions are adequate.

items.situation: A turbine-powered aircraft (turbojet) loses communications in delegated Canadian airspace after departing Toronto. The aircraft was cleared to a fix 150 NM short of its destination, Winnipeg. The route continues across flat terrain before reaching U.S. airspace approximately 50 NM after the clearance limit fix. Another controller plans for the aircraft to remain at the clearance-limit altitude all the way to Winnipeg because radio-failure aircraft must hold their last altitude to destination.
items.question_text: How should you plan control actions for this aircraft's altitude profile?

choices.text: Plan for the aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitude(s) or flight level(s). Because U.S. airspace is within 10 minutes of the clearance limit (50 NM in a turbojet), the climb to border-crossing altitude will commence at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.
Plan for the aircraft to hold the last assigned altitude all the way to Winnipeg, because all radio-failure aircraft maintain clearance-limit altitude to destination.
Plan only for the 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit — the border-crossing rule does not apply because the destination is in Canada, not the U.S.
Plan for immediate climb to filed altitude after the clearance limit because the terrain is flat and there is no safety concern.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-5: (1) maintain last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever higher) until 10 min beyond clearance limit, then filed altitudes; (2) if U.S. airspace is within 10 min after the clearance limit, climb to border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the boundary. Both rules apply here.

items.situation: A pilot requests SVFR at a Canadian control zone where no airport controller is on duty. The official weather report shows a 900-foot ceiling and 2 miles visibility. ATC currently has one IFR arrival expected in 15 minutes. Another controller says to approve the request because the ceiling and visibility are within minima and IFR traffic alone shouldn't block SVFR.
items.question_text: How should the SVFR request be evaluated in this situation?

choices.text: Evaluate based on BOTH the current or anticipated IFR traffic AND the official ceiling and visibility reports. Both factors together determine the decision.
Evaluate based on ceiling and visibility only, because without a controller on duty, IFR traffic is not a criterion.
Evaluate based on IFR traffic only, because without a controller the criteria are the same as when one is present.
Deny the request automatically because SVFR is never authorized when no airport controller is on duty.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-7b, when no airport controller is on duty, the SVFR decision must consider both current or anticipated IFR traffic AND official ceiling and visibility reports. Neither factor alone is sufficient.
